papers.title: Shared neural substrates of prosocial and parenting behaviours
papers.authors: Fangmiao Sun, Kayla Y. Lim, James Dang, Li I. Zhang, Ye Emily Wu et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: This study found shared neural substrates in the brain for prosocial and parenting behaviors, suggesting a common neural basis for these behaviors.

papers.title: Heart surgery with quick-setting magnetic fluid could prevent strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe, Shamini Bundell
papers.summary: Researchers have developed a quick-setting magnetic fluid that could prevent strokes by filling the area where blood clots form, and have also created a form of diamond in the lab.

papers.title: Magnetic gel injected into the heart could stop strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe
papers.summary: A hydrogel-based plug injected into the heart has been shown to be effective in reducing the risk of stroke in animal trials.

papers.title: Genome modelling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2
papers.authors: Garyk Brixi, Matthew G. Durrant, Jerome Ku, Mohsen Naghipourfar, Michael Poli et al. (+57 more)
papers.summary: Evo 2 is an artificial intelligence-based model that predicts functional properties from genomic sequences and provides a rich generative model for researchers in biology.

papers.title: DICER cleavage fidelity is governed by 5′-end binding pockets
papers.authors: Minh Khoa Ngo, Cong Truc Le, Tuan Anh Nguyen
papers.summary: Researchers have used cryo-electron microscopy and massively parallel assays to shed light on the mechanism by which the DICER enzyme cleaves RNA at precise locations to produce small RNAs.

papers.title: Limited thermal tolerance in tropical insects and its genomic signature
papers.authors: Kim L. Holzmann, Thomas Schmitzer, Antonia Abels, Marko Čorkalo, Oliver Mitesser et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: A study of tropical insects found that they have limited thermal tolerance, meaning they can't survive high temperatures. This is due to the fundamental properties of their protein architecture. This has significant implications for understanding how insects adapt to changing environments.

papers.title: A metabolic alarmin from keratinocytes potentiates systemic humoral immunity
papers.authors: Zhenglin Ji, Ji Gao, Shaocun Zhang, Jiajie Li, Haijing Wu et al. (+39 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a new metabolic alarmin, farnesyl pyrophosphate, that enhances the immune system by stimulating the production of antibodies. This alarmin is produced by keratinocytes and has significant implications for understanding how the immune system is regulated.

papers.title: Long-term thrombus-free left atrial appendage occlusion via magnetofluids
papers.authors: Shu Wang, Wenhao Ju, Donglin Zhuang, Zhecheng Chen, Dongliang Zhao et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: Scientists developed a new method for occluding the left atrial appendage using magnetofluids, which can resist high-speed blood flow. This method has the potential to be personalized and complete, offering a new strategy for left atrial appendage occlusion.

papers.title: Lipid metabolism drives dietary effects on T cell ferroptosis and immunity
papers.authors: Naiqi Wang, Zhian Chen, Yin Yao, Chenglong Sun, Wei Wei et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: A study found that lipid metabolism plays a crucial role in regulating T cell ferroptosis and immunity. The balance of systemic polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty acids determines the susceptibility of T cells to ferroptosis, highlighting the importance of dietary composition in regulating T cell function.

papers.title: Merlin: a computed tomography vision–language foundation model and dataset
papers.authors: Louis Blankemeier, Ashwin Kumar, Joseph Paul Cohen, Jiaming Liu, Longchao Liu et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: Researchers developed Merlin, a computed tomography vision–language foundation model and dataset, which demonstrates strong performance across various tasks and external sites. Merlin was trained on a large dataset of paired CT scans, patient record data, and radiology reports.

papers.title: Microbiota-mediated induction of beige adipocytes in response to dietary cues
papers.authors: Takeshi Tanoue, Manabu Nagayama, Ayumi J. A. Roochana, Samuel Zimmerman, Orr Ashenberg et al. (+28 more)
papers.summary: A low-protein diet in mice leads to a change in their fat tissue, with the help of gut bacteria, to burn energy like brown fat.

papers.title: Mechanism of co-transcriptional cap snatching by influenza polymerase
papers.authors: Alexander Helmut Rotsch, Delong Li, Maud Dupont, Tim Krischuns, Ute Neef et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have discovered how the influenza virus takes a piece of the host's RNA to help it replicate.

papers.title: Cell-free chromatin state tracing reveals disease origin and therapy responses
papers.authors: Xubin Chen, Xiaoxuan Meng, Weilong Zhang, Xiawei Zhang, Yaping Zhang et al. (+26 more)
papers.summary: A new method for analyzing DNA in the blood can help diagnose diseases and understand where they come from.

papers.title: The oldest articulated bony fish from the early Silurian period
papers.authors: You-An Zhu, Yang Chen, Qiang Li, Wen-Jin Zhao, Zheng-Da Zhou et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Fossil discovery of the oldest known bony fish, dating back to the early Silurian period, provides new insights into the evolution of fish.

papers.title: Bulk hexagonal diamond
papers.authors: Shoulong Lai, Xigui Yang, Jiuyang Shi, Shijie Liu, Ying Guo et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have successfully synthesized millimeter-sized hexagonal diamond, a new form of diamond, by compressing graphite under high pressure and temperature.

papers.title: Sea level much higher than assumed in most coastal hazard assessments
papers.authors: Katharina Seeger, Philip S. J. Minderhoud
papers.summary: A new study found that sea levels are higher than previously assumed in most coastal hazard assessments, which could impact coastal communities and infrastructure.

papers.title: A glucocorticoid–FAS axis controls immune evasion during metastatic seeding
papers.authors: Monica Cassandras, Xavier Sanchez, Lauren Hsu, Yu Huang, Adam J. Getzler et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a key driver of resistance to cancer treatment, which could lead to new therapeutic strategies for triple-negative breast cancer.

papers.title: The molecular basis of force selectivity by PIEZO2
papers.authors: Eric M. Mulhall, Oleg Yarishkin, Rose Z. Hill, Anna K. Koster, Ardem Patapoutian
papers.summary: Scientists uncovered the molecular basis of force selectivity by PIEZO2, a protein that senses mechanical stimuli, which could lead to new treatments for various diseases.

papers.title: Advancing operational global aerosol forecasting with machine learning
papers.authors: Ke Gui, Xutao Zhang, Huizheng Che, Lei Li, Yu Zheng et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: A new machine-learning-driven forecasting system was developed to improve operational global aerosol forecasting, which could lead to better air quality predictions.

papers.title: Wind shear enhances soil moisture influence on rapid thunderstorm growth
papers.authors: Christopher M. Taylor, Cornelia Klein, Emma J. Barton, Sebastian Hahn, Wolfgang Wagner
papers.summary: Researchers found that wind shear amplifies the impact of soil moisture on triggering rapidly developing thunderstorms, which could improve weather forecasting models.

papers.title: Structural basis of RNA-guided transcription by a dCas12f–σ<sup>E</sup>–RNAP complex
papers.authors: Renjian Xiao, Florian T. Hoffmann, Dan Xie, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: Researchers used cryogenic electron microscopy to study how a dCas12f protein recruits RNA polymerase to targeted DNA, initiating transcription. This process bypasses traditional recognition elements and stabilizes the transcription start site. The study provides insights into RNA-guided transcription.

papers.title: Homologous recombination deficiency and hemizygosity drive resistance in breast cancer
papers.authors: Anton Safonov, Minna Lee, David N. Brown, Luca Boscolo Bielo, Miika Mehine et al. (+52 more)
papers.summary: Researchers identified genomic patterns that drive resistance to therapy in breast cancer. Germline and somatic interactions play a key role in defining actionable genomic patterns. This study provides insights into acquired therapy resistance in breast cancer.

papers.title: Exapted CRISPR–Cas12f homologues drive RNA-guided transcription
papers.authors: Florian T. Hoffmann, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri, Juniper Glass-Klaiber, Renjian Xiao et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered that CRISPR-Cas12f homologues can drive RNA-guided transcription. Specialized σ factors interact with nuclease-dead CRISPR-Cas12f proteins to form potent gene activation systems. This study provides insights into RNA-guided gene activation.

papers.title: Wide-swath altimetry maps bank shapes and storage changes in global rivers
papers.authors: A. Cerbelaud, J. Wade, C. H. David, M. Durand, R. P. M. Frasson et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: Researchers used satellite data to map river channel geometry and water storage changes worldwide. The study provides insights into global river dynamics and water storage changes.

papers.title: Largest Silurian fish illuminates the origin of osteichthyan characters
papers.authors: Jing Lu, Brian Choo, Wenjin Zhao, You-an Zhu, Xindong Cui et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a new Silurian fish species that provides insights into the origin of osteichthyan characters. The study provides new morphological details of a stem osteichthyan.

papers.title: Precancerous niche remodelling dictates nascent tumour persistence
papers.authors: G. Skrupskelyte, J. E. Rojo Arias, H. Ajith, Y. Dang, D. Rossetti et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: A study found that a specific type of niche in the stomach can support the survival of nascent tumors. This niche is created by fibroblasts remodeling the stroma in response to tumor stress. The researchers suggest that this could be a key factor in the development of certain types of cancer.

papers.title: Rising temperatures pose a threat to tropical insects
papers.authors: Vojtech Novotny
papers.summary: An assessment of insect species in mountainous terrain found that rising temperatures pose a threat to tropical insects, potentially leading to biodiversity loss in lowland areas.

papers.title: Fresh claim of making elusive ‘hexagonal’ diamond is the strongest yet
papers.authors: Mark Peplow
papers.summary: Researchers claim to have found evidence of the existence of 'hexagonal' diamond, a rare form of carbon that has been debated for decades.

papers.title: Stress-hormone signalling protects spreading cancer cells from immune system
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Analysis revealed that cancer cells can resist killing by the immune system by harnessing stress-hormone signalling through the glucocorticoid receptor. This could be a potential therapeutic target for preventing metastasis.

papers.title: AI can write genomes — how long until it creates synthetic life?
papers.authors: Ewen Callaway
papers.summary: A genomic language model called Evo2 can generate short genome sequences, but scientists say further advances are needed to write genomes that will work inside living cells.

papers.title: Magnetic fluid offers better seal in heart-plugging medical procedure
papers.authors: Yu Shrike Zhang
papers.summary: Researchers have developed an injectable fluid that can be used to close off part of the heart in animals, potentially improving a procedure that prevents stroke in people with irregular heartbeats.

papers.title: Funding from individual donors: lessons from the Epstein case
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Universities need to establish and empower compliance teams to ensure adherence to ethical funding policies, particularly in light of the Epstein case.

papers.title: A genetic switch turns off parental behaviour and drives infanticide in male striped mice
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Researchers have identified a genetic switch in male striped mice that affects their behavior towards pups, with high expression of the Agouti gene leading to hostility.

papers.title: Do wet or dry soils trigger thunderstorms? It depends on how the wind blows
papers.authors: Craig R. Ferguson
papers.summary: Satellite data show that wind conditions affect the connection between soil moisture and thunderstorms, which could be used to inform forecasting.

papers.title: Climate change and geopolitics threaten water supplies — but disaster is not inevitable
papers.authors: Ali Tauqeer Sheikh
papers.summary: The Indus Waters Treaty has withstood several challenges, including armed conflicts and glacier loss, and could serve as a blueprint for other water management agreements.

papers.title: Cancer blood tests are everywhere. Do they really work?
papers.authors: Nic Fleming
papers.summary: A review of cancer blood tests raises concerns about their effectiveness in detecting cancer types and delivering accurate diagnoses.

papers.title: Skin cells boost distant antibody responses
papers.authors: Xueping Zhu, Caroline L. Sokol
papers.summary: Researchers have discovered a pathway in skin cells that boosts the production of antibodies to protect the body from infection.

papers.title: How a mathematician is cracking open Mexico’s powerful drug cartels

papers.summary: A mathematician has developed models to help combat organized crime in Mexico, which could improve public safety.

papers.title: Under pressure: the reality of Mexico’s research system

papers.summary: A researcher speaks out about the unstable conditions faced by Mexican scientists and proposes solutions to improve their situation.

papers.title: Radiology AI makes consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centres
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A 3D vision-language model called Merlin has been trained to make consistent diagnoses using radiology images from different health centers.

papers.title: Hunt for reactive metabolites uncovers unusual chemistry in a human pathogen
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A new method was developed to capture and identify reactive compounds produced by a human pathogen, leading to the discovery of two unusual molecules with potential for broad synthetic applications.

papers.authors: André Pinassi Antunes, Pedro de Araujo Lima Constantino, Julia E. Fa, Daniel P. Munari, Thais Q. Morcatty et al. (+53 more)
papers.summary: A correction was made to a previous study on the importance of healthy forests in safeguarding traditional wild meat food systems in Amazonia.

papers.summary: A genetically encoded assembly recorder was developed to temporally resolve cellular history, allowing for a deeper understanding of cellular processes.

papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A star system consisting of four Sun-like stars was discovered to be the most compact of its kind, with all four stars fitting within the area of Jupiter's orbit.

papers.summary: A step-by-step approach to releasing open-weight AI was proposed to alleviate risks associated with its development and deployment.

papers.summary: This paper is not a scientific study, but rather a historical and future-focused article on telecommunications.

papers.title: South Korea’s AI framework act focuses on rights and safety
papers.authors: WooJung Jon
papers.summary: South Korea's AI framework act prioritizes rights and safety, but details of the act are not provided.

papers.authors: Zhenhua Rui, Lu Lin, Hongfang Lu, Y. Frank Cheng
papers.summary: Climate research is a global effort, and risks and responsibilities should be distributed accordingly.

papers.title: Why ‘quantum proteins’ could be the next big thing in biology

papers.summary: Fluorescent proteins with quantum upgrades could offer unprecedented views inside cells, potentially revolutionizing biology.

papers.title: US climate actions must continue, despite setbacks
papers.authors: David Widawsky
papers.summary: Despite setbacks, US climate actions must continue, with cities, states, and businesses taking the lead.

papers.title: My relationship with my PhD supervisor has become toxic — what do I do?

papers.summary: A graduate student in China has become seriously depressed after facing frequent verbal abuse from their PhD supervisor.

papers.title: How infighting led the Maya civilization to catastrophic collapse

papers.summary: Fresh archaeological evidence suggests that infighting led to the catastrophic collapse of the Maya civilization.

papers.title: See raining iguanas and coral from the inside out — February’s best science images

papers.summary: Nature's photo team selects the month's sharpest science shots, including images of raining iguanas and coral.

papers.summary: Climate change may affect malaria control efforts in Africa by reshaping malaria risk, but extreme weather could be a more immediate danger.

papers.authors: Gabriel Castrillo, Paulo José Pereira Lima Teixeira, Sur Herrera Paredes, Theresa F. Law, Laura de Lorenzo et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: This is a correction to a previous paper on the role of root microbiota in integrating phosphate stress and immunity.

papers.authors: Christopher W. Koo, Jiaqi Xiao, Sebastien Coassolo, Jie Liu, Christine Yu et al. (+8 more)

papers.title: The squeal of peeling tape, explained
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: The sound of peeling tape is caused by weak shock waves triggered by micro-cracks in the adhesive layer.

papers.title: Audio long read: Many people have no mental imagery. What’s going on in their brains?

papers.summary: Aphantasia is a condition where people have no mental imagery. Researchers are studying the brains of people with aphantasia to understand consciousness.

papers.title: The hidden lives behind China’s great Internet firewall

papers.summary: The digital ecosystem in China is tightly controlled, but there are human stories and acts of resistance behind the scenes.

papers.title: AI agents are ‘aeroplanes for the mind’: five ways to ensure that scientists are responsible pilots
papers.authors: Dashun Wang
papers.summary: As AI systems take on more scientific tasks, researchers should focus on designing platforms that preserve creativity and responsibility.

papers.title: Earth’s oldest crystals suggest an early start for plate tectonics
papers.authors: Alexandra Witze
papers.summary: Earth's oldest crystals suggest that plate tectonics may have started 3.3 billion years ago.

papers.title: ‘No one quite like her’: meet the female colleagues who inspire these award-winning women in science
papers.authors: Amanda Heidt
papers.summary: Women in science are nominating their female colleagues who inspire them, highlighting the importance of female mentorship and support.

papers.title: Editorial Expression of Concern: The gene product Murr1 restricts HIV-1 replication in resting CD4+ lymphocytes

papers.summary: Nature staff discuss the week's top science news, including Pokémon's 30th anniversary.

papers.title: Brain mysteries and Bronze Age diplomacy: Books in brief

papers.title: White House stalls release of approved US science budgets
papers.authors: Max Kozlov, Alexandra Witze, Dan Garisto
papers.summary: The US Congress rejected sweeping cuts to science agencies, but the NIH, NSF, and NASA have had their spending slowed.

papers.title: I will continue the fight for environmental justice in Black communities
papers.authors: Virginia Gewin
papers.summary: Robert Bullard argues that researchers in environmental justice have a responsibility to back up their work with action.

papers.title: Pokémon turns 30 — how the fictional pocket monsters shaped science
papers.authors: Miryam Naddaf
papers.summary: The Pokémon franchise has inspired generations of researchers in various fields, including evolution, biodiversity, and research integrity.

papers.title: Is a ‘selfish gene’ making a Utah family have twice as many boys as girls?

papers.summary: Researchers claim to have found the first conclusive case of sex 'distortion' in humans, and also discuss how stem cells could treat spina bifida and the influence of Pokémon on science.

papers.authors: Sanjeev S. Ranade, Feiya Li, Sean Whalen, Angelo Pelonero, Lin Ye et al. (+18 more)

papers.summary: Pyruvate was found to suppress interferon signaling by inducing STAT1 protein pyruvylation, a post-translational modification that can modulate immune signaling and host antiviral response.

papers.authors: Alexandra Rundberg Nilsson
papers.summary: TNF's effects on hematopoiesis are more complex than previously thought, and it can have both positive and negative effects depending on the cell type, context, and time.

papers.summary: Researchers have successfully reconstituted the cellular niche required for primordial germ cell-like cells to mature in humans, but these cells are prone to dedifferentiation.

papers.authors: Hitesh Chopra, Yuanyuan Han
papers.summary: The concept of 'regenesis' is proposed as a new framework for describing hybrid tissue outcomes in stem cell therapeutics, aiming to update terminology in the field.

papers.authors: Panpan Shi, Kairang Jin, Guoxing Yin, Lin Liu

papers.summary: ASCL1 promotes nuclear shrinkage in transdifferentiation by suppressing NUP37, a critical component of the nuclear pore complex, and knockdown of NUP37 enhances ASCL1-mediated transdifferentiation.
